coredata.dc:title: Cellulose nanofiber-based coating film integrated with nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots for active packaging applications of fresh fruit
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Molaei, Rahim
Priyadarshi, Ruchir
Han, Sanghee
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with glucose as the carbon source (GCD) and functionalized with nitrogen. The GCD and N-functionalized carbon dots (NGCD) were used to prepare cellulose nanofiber (CNF)-based composite films. GCD and NGCD provided high UV blocking properties to the CNF film without changing the transparency, reducing the T280 by 91–98 % and T660 by 12−10 %. The addition of GCD and NGCD did not affect the mechanical properties but increased the film's water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA). The composite films also showed high antioxidant activity with 99−99 % of ABTS and 80–85 % of DPPH radical scavenging activities. The CNF-based films with GCD generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) to exhibit high antimicrobial activity; however, the CNF/NGCD film showed stronger antibacterial and antifungal activity than the CNF/GCD film. In addition, the CNF-based films incorporated with GCDs showed negligible cytotoxicity. When tangerine and strawberry fruit were coated with the CNF-based coating solutions, the fruit coated with CNF/NGCD inhibited fungal growth on the fruit surface, extending their shelf life by more than 10 and 2 d, respectively. The CD-added CNF films with high antioxidant, antimicrobial, and low cytotoxicity are likely to be used for antifungal coating of fresh fruit and active packaging applications.

head.ce:title: Cellulose nanofiber-based coating film integrated with nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots for active packaging applications of fresh fruit

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with glucose as the carbon source (GCD) and functionalized with nitrogen. The GCD and N-functionalized carbon dots (NGCD) were used to prepare cellulose nanofiber (CNF)-based composite films. GCD and NGCD provided high UV blocking properties to the CNF film without changing the transparency, reducing the T280 by 91–98 % and T660 by 12−10 %. The addition of GCD and NGCD did not affect the mechanical properties but increased the film's water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA). The composite films also showed high antioxidant activity with 99−99 % of ABTS and 80–85 % of DPPH radical scavenging activities. The CNF-based films with GCD generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) to exhibit high antimicrobial activity; however, the CNF/NGCD film showed stronger antibacterial and antifungal activity than the CNF/GCD film. In addition, the CNF-based films incorporated with GCDs showed negligible cytotoxicity. When tangerine and strawberry fruit were coated with the CNF-based coating solutions, the fruit coated with CNF/NGCD inhibited fungal growth on the fruit surface, extending their shelf life by more than 10 and 2 d, respectively. The CD-added CNF films with high antioxidant, antimicrobial, and low cytotoxicity are likely to be used for antifungal coating of fresh fruit and active packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Most fruit are highly perishable and rapidly undergo postharvest deterioration. Thus, preventing such quality degradation during storage and distribution is a fundamental challenge for marketing from an economic and technical point of view (Boonkorn et al., 2012). The postharvest life of fruit is limited due to high moisture content and ongoing biological processes after harvesting, making them vulnerable to physical injuries, physiological disorders, and mainly microbial infections (Xu et al., 2018). Even though various preservation methods such as chemical treatment (Tzortzakis and Chrysargyris, 2017), blanching (Xin et al., 2015), UV-irradiation (Turtoi, 2013), plasma treatment (Giannoglou et al., 2021), modified atmosphere packaging (Li et al., 2017; Priyadarshi et al., 2020), active packaging films and coating (Davoodi et al., 2020; Ezati et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2019), and so on have been used to extend the shelf life of fruit, the quest for newer technologies continue for further betterment.

Currently, biopolymer-based active thin films and coatings are at the forefront of state-of-the-art technologies for efficient microbial load reduction and maintaining the quality of fresh fruit and vegetables. The development of active edible films and coatings can act in several ways to preserve fresh produce. They protect the food from microbial contamination, which is the most common reason for food spoilage (Davoodi et al., 2020). Also, the functional coatings can act as UV-barrier materials, helping prevent UV-mediated oxidation of food (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a). Besides this, the coating helps regulate the gas exchange between the environment and the fresh produce, which controls the respiration rate and delays ripening and spoilage (Sapper and Chiralt, 2018). Hence, coatings increase the shelf life and aid the long-term storage of fresh or processed foods. Also, biopolymer coatings can provide an aesthetic appearance to foods while retaining moisture and flavor (Lotfi et al., 2018). Of the various biopolymers available for this purpose, cellulose is one of the best candidates because it is abundant, economical, and safe with its excellent film-forming ability (Ezati et al., 2021a). In particular, nanofibrillated cellulose or cellulose nanofiber (CNF) obtained from cellulose is highly likely to be used as an edible coating on fruit and vegetables because of its ability to form transparent, glossy films with high gas barrier properties, which are desirable characteristics for this application (Isogai and Bergström, 2018). Besides, CNF films possess decent intrinsic physicochemical properties and can be stably incorporated with various functional materials, providing antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV protection properties (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021).

Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots (CDs) have recently been introduced as a new class of nanomaterials with photoluminescent properties due to their small particle size (< 10 nm) (Kouloumpis et al., 2017). CD was first identified in 2004 during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes (Shaik et al., 2021). Since then, an easy and efficient CD synthesis method using a simple carbon source such as glucose has been developed and is widely used in various fields as an economical and abundant functional material (Cailotto et al., 2018; Priyadarshi et al., 2021b). CDs are composed of a chemical structure with a central carbon core and functional groups at the edges, and the type of functional groups in CDs can vary depending on the carbon source used (Shaik et al., 2021). For example, CDs obtained from a combination of citric acid and urea have nitrogen functional groups at the edges (Ji et al., 2020). So, inter-conjugation of the core carbon dots with heteroatoms like non-metallic elements such as N, Si, B, S, and metals such as Ag, Mn, Mg, etc., can provide core CDs with new functionalities, thus extending the scope of their application (Emam and Ahmed, 2021). The presence of nitrogen-based functional groups such as amines has long been known to provide unexpected functions to the base material (Shaik et al., 2021; Travlou et al., 2018). Functionalizing CDs with nitrogen, a safe and abundant element, enhances CDs' antioxidant and antibacterial properties, making them a potent functional material (Ezati et al., 2021c; Roy et al., 2021).

Currently, CDs are mainly used in optoelectronics and biomedical areas, including biological imaging, optical devices, photocatalysis, drug delivery, and biochemical detection (Lim et al., 2015) due to their beneficial properties such as low cost, easy synthesis, high water solubility, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and stable photoluminescence (Wang et al., 2017). Therefore, the main objective of the present study was to explore the potential of CD as a functional antioxidant and antimicrobial material for active packaging applications. For this, functional CNF-based films were prepared by incorporating two types of CDs (GCD and NGCD) and evaluated the physicochemical and functional properties of the film. In addition, the efficacy of the functional film was verified through the edible coating of tangerines and strawberries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: CD was prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method using an aqueous solution of glucose and urea (Cheng et al., 2019). For the preparation of N-functionalized glucose carbon dots (NGCD), 1 g of glucose and 0.5 g of urea were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water with stirring and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL) followed by the reactor was heated to 200 °C for 6 h in an oil bath. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged for 30 min to remove the unreacted material. To remove impurities, the supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size: 0.22 μm, Germany). After that, the filtrate was freeze-dried to obtain a black powder of CD and stored in a refrigerator before further testing. Glucose carbon dots (GCD) were also prepared using the same procedure without adding urea for comparison. The prepared carbon dots were characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

CNF-based films were prepared using a solution casting method. For this, 4 g of CNF and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 150 mL of distilled water with vigorous stirring at 80 °C for 30 min. Afterward, 0.04 g of GCD or NGCD (1 wt % based on polymer) were added slowly to the solution with vigorous stirring for one hour. The film-forming solution was promptly cast onto a leveled Teflon film (Cole-Parmer Instrument Co., Chicago, IL, USA) coated glass plate (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at 25 °C for 2 d. The dried film was peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % RH for at least 2 d before further testing. For comparison, a neat CNF film was prepared using the same procedure without GCD addition. The prepared films were designated CNF, CNF/GCD, and CNF/NGCD according to the type of filler. The film properties were characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

The microstructure of the composite film is primarily influenced by the compatibility between the filler molecules and the polymer matrix and the distribution of fillers within the polymer matrix. The surface and cross-sectional FE-SEM images of the CNF-based films are shown in Fig. 1 . The film's surface morphology shows a smooth, continuous structure of cellulose nanofibers without cracks or defects. The carbon dots (GCD and NGCD) were uniformly dispersed in the polymer showing the even distribution of the CDs within the CNF polymer matrix. The continuous internal structure of the composite film has also been demonstrated in cross-sectional images, which are essential for composite film characterization (You et al., 2016).

The CNF-based films were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy to investigate the chemical interactions and changes in the CNF film by adding the CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 a. In the neat CNF film, the characteristic peaks of CNF at 3335, 2892, 1602 cm−1 and the shoulder bands between 1350−1390 cm−1 were observed, which were due to the vibration of the OH, CH, and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of the CO, and CH
scissoring motion of cellulose, respectively (Ezati et al., 2020). The band around 1407 cm−1 represents the OH bending vibration of carboxylic acid, and 1160 cm−1 was associated with asymmetric stretching of the COC in pyranose ring (Soni et al., 2015). The peaks at 1107 and 1030 cm−1 can be attributed to the CC ring stretching band and the CO stretching of CNF, respectively (Ventura-Cruz and Tecante, 2019). Other peaks observed at 860 and 900 cm−1 were related to the β-glycosidic linkages in the cellulosic structure of CNF. The CD-added CNF films showed cellulosic fingerprint region, and other characteristic peaks were similar to the neat CNF film. However, the stretching vibration of carboxylic groups of the CNF substrate (1602 cm−1) in the GCD and NGCD-added CNF films shifted to 1603 and 1604 cm−1, respectively, with a noticeable shift related to the OH bending vibration (around 1410 cm−1) to higher wavenumbers in the composite films, indicating that the CDs were embedded in the film through hydrogen bonding with the carboxylic acid groups of CNF (Ahn et al., 2021; El-Shamy and Zayied, 2020).

XRD analysis was used to study the phase compositions of CNF-based polymers, and the results are shown in Fig. 2b. The XRD patterns of the CNF, CNF/GCD, and CNF/NGCD films showed two diffraction peaks at 15.6 ° and 23.3 °, typical (110) and (002) diffraction of cellulose I. These results revealed the semi-crystalline properties of the CNF polymer (Jiang et al., 2016; Roy and Rhim, 2021). Compared with the XRD patterns of the neat CNF film, CNF/GCD and CNF/NGCD films showed identical diffraction patterns without additional peaks, indicating a homogeneous distribution of CDs within the polymer matrix. It may be because the amount of CD used to prepare the composite film was insufficient to form the crystallized structure in the composite film (Koshy et al., 2021). The uniform dispersion of CDs in composite films can be related to the amorphous and non-crystalline properties of the CDs (Kandra and Bajpai, 2020).

Elemental mapping and EDX were performed to determine the elemental composition of the CNF/NGCD films (Fig. 2c–d). The nitrogen distribution on the surface of CD was confirmed using elemental mapping of SEM images. The composition of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) of NGCD was 79.3 %, 17.3 %, and 3.4 %, respectively. Fig. 2c shows the EDX spectra of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) in the CNF matrix, where it can be seen that nitrogen (N) appeared by the addition of NGCD. Mapping images showed that all three elements (C, O, N) appeared in the CNF film containing nitrogen-functionalized CDs (NGCDs), with low N content and evenly distributed between C and O.

The color of food packaging film is related to the quality of the packaged food and is a key factor in determining the consumer's first impression of a product. The surface color and light transmittance of the CNF-based films are shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information). The neat CNF film was highly transparent without color, indicating a high L-value (brightness) with high light transmittance (86.7 % of T660) and low a and b-values. The addition of GCD and NGCD reduced the CNF film's brightness while increasing the redness (a-value) and blueness (b-value). As a result, the total color difference (ΔE) of the film increased, mainly due to the brown color of the CD, which increased the redness and yellowness in the CD-added films.

The UV barrier and transparency properties of the film were evaluated by determining the light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660), respectively, and the results are shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information). The T280 of the CNF film was considerably reduced from 53.8 % to 5.1 % and 1.3 %, respectively, by the addition of the GCD and NGCD, while the T660 decreased slightly from 86.7 % to 80.2 % and 78.3 %. Interestingly, the addition of CD increased the UV blocking properties without substantially reducing the transparency of the CNF film. The UV barrier property of the CD-added films was due to the UV-light absorption property of the CD, which showed maximum light absorption at 295 nm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). In general, light promotes the decomposition and oxidation of nutrients in food through the formation of various chemicals, causing off-flavor, rancidity, discoloration, and producing toxic compounds (Ezati and Rhim, 2021). As one of the main functions of the packaging film, light-barrier property, especially UV-blocking, has a vital function to prevent deterioration of food quality caused by the photochemical reaction (Kwon et al., 2018). As a result, developed films are advantageous for maintaining transparency and providing UV protection. The transparency of active food packaging films plays an important role mainly in two respects. The transparent packaging film allows consumers to directly observe the contents and evaluate the quality, whereas the transparent film can reduce the quality of food being sensitive to photochemical reactions. In particular, in some photosensitive foods, the product's shelf life can be extended by using a light-shielding packaging film that blocks the light of a specific wavelength and prevents deterioration due to photochemical reaction (Vilela et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and water contact angle (WCA) of the film
ce:section.ce:para: Packaging films must meet adequate mechanical strength and integrity to protect food products during storage and distribution by preventing breakage or leakage (Ezati and Rhim, 2021). Changes in the physical properties of the CD-added films are shown in Table 1 . The thickness of the film is an important parameter that affects other properties of the active film, such as mechanical properties and barrier properties. It is usually affected by the type of polymer matrix and the amount of filler added. However, there was no difference in the thickness of the CNF film by adding CDs, which may be due to the uniform distribution of the filler without aggregation in the polymer matrix. Tensile properties (TS, EB, and EM) of CNF films indicate that CNF films are relatively strong (high TS of 73−75 MPa) and stiff (high EM of about 6 GPa) with lower flexibility (low EB of about 4 %) and much stronger than other biopolymer-based films such as chitosan, carrageenan, and agar (Azizi-Lalabadi et al., 2020; Ezati and Rhim, 2020a, 2020b). The tensile properties of the CNF film were not changed by the addition of GCD and NGCD.

The surface wettability or hydrophilicity of the CNF-based films was evaluated by measuring water contact angle (WCA). Usually, a biopolymer-based film with a WCA is less than 65 ° is considered to have a hydrophilic surface (Ezati et al., 2020). The WCA of the neat CNF film was 30.1 ° (Table 1), indicating that the CNF film is highly hydrophilic. Though the difference was not statistically major, the WCA of the CNF film was slightly decreased by the addition of GCDs due to the hydrophilicity of the CDs. In general, it is known that the surface hydrophobicity of biopolymer-based films is determined by the properties of polymers and additives and their interaction mode (Roy and Rhim, 2020). As observed in the present results, the addition of hydrophilic fillers is expected to reduce the hydrophobicity of the resulting composite film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP), oxygen transfer rate (OTR), water solubility (WS), and swelling ratio (SR) of the film
ce:section.ce:para: Packaging films with low WVP values are desirable for maintaining food quality and extending the shelf life of packaged foods when their quality changes due to moisture movement between the food and the surrounding environment and degradation of the packaged food during storage and distribution are accelerated (Yadav et al., 2020). The WVP of a composite film is affected by the type and interaction of the filler and polymer matrix. A series of physical processes, such as adsorption of water vapor on the film surface, diffusion of water molecules through the film, and evaporation of water on the other side of the film, are involved in the water vapor transmission of the film (Ezati and Rhim, 2021; Yadav et al., 2020). The WVP of the neat CNF was 0.55 × 10−9 g.m/m2.Pa.s. The WVP of the CNF film was not changed when GCD was added but increased when NGCD was incorporated (Table 1). The increased WVP of the NGCD-added CNF film maybe because NGCD is more hydrophilic than GCD and the WVP results agree well with the WCA test results.

The oxygen transmission rate (OTR) of the CNF-based films is also shown in Table 1. The addition of CD (GCD and NGCD) reduced the OTR of the CNF film. The improved oxygen barrier properties of the composite film may be due to the additional interfacial bonding between the GCD and CNF polymers, which provides a denser structure with reduced free volume (Kim et al., 2020). Similarly, various research results have reported that the biopolymer-based film's oxygen permeability is reduced by adding nanomaterials (Kim et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2018). In conclusion, the film helps regulate gas exchange between fresh produce and the environment, delaying ripening and spoilage by controlling respiration rates (Sapper and Chiralt, 2018).

WS and SR are essential properties of food packaging films applied to high moisture or moisture-sensitive foods. The WS and SR of the CNF-based films are shown in Fig. 3 . The WS of the neat CNF film was 18.5 and considerably increased to 25.1 and 24.8 by adding GDC and NGCD, respectively. The increased WS of the GCD-added films was due to the hydrophilic nature of the GCD. On the other hand, the SR of the film was not changed by the addition of GDC and NGCD. The water-resistance properties like WS and SR are expected to affect the releasing rate of functional materials and the resulting functional properties such as antimicrobial and antioxidant activities of the composite films.

The release of CD from the GCD and NGCD-incorporated CNF films was evaluated using food simulant solutions, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. The release rate of the GCD was dependent on the food simulant solution and the type of GCD. When the CNF/GCD film was immersed in water, GCD was released rapidly during the first 30 min, then the release rate decreased, and after 3 h, it reached a steady state. On the other hand, the release rate in the alcohol solution was much lower than that in water depending on the alcohol concentration; that is, the release rate decreased as the alcohol concentration increased. The release of GCD was moderate in 10 % and 50 % ethanol, but the release rate was very low in 95 % ethanol. The release rate is affected by the solubility of the polymer matrix and the affinity between the solvent and the GCD. In addition to the hydrophilicity of GCD, aqueous solutions readily penetrate the hydrophilic CNF matrix and are sufficiently hydrated to form a more open structure, releasing the GCD quickly.

In contrast, alcohols have limited access to CNF polymers, resulting in GCD trapped in the polymer matrix and reducing diffusion and mobility in closed structures. Therefore, GCD was released faster and more in water than in alcoholic solutions. On the other hand, the release of NGCD was faster and more than GCD in all test solutions. Like GCD, NGCD was released faster in water than in alcoholic solutions. However, in the 10 % alcohol solution, the release of NGCD was higher than that of GCD, tracing its release pattern in the water.

Interestingly, NGCD showed higher release than GCD in alcoholic solutions, which may be due to nitrogen compounds such as amines and amides in NGCD. These complexes are less polar due to the chelating of cations and lead to the high lipophilicity and solubility of NGCD in alcohol solutions (Caneschi et al., 2017; El-Ayaan and Abdel-Aziz, 2005). The increased release rate of GCD is expected to improve the functional properties of the film when it comes into contact with food.

The antioxidant activity of the CNF-based films was evaluated using ABTS and DPPH methods, which are the most commonly used for assessing the antioxidant function of packaging films. ABTS and DPPH are long-lived, containing free radicals that change color when interacting with an antioxidant from deep green and purple to colorless or yellow/colorless. The result of the antioxidant activity of the films is shown in Fig. 3. The neat CNF film showed some antioxidant activity of 14.1 % and 5.8 % ABTS and DPPH scavenging efficacy, attributed to the hydroxyl group of CNF that acts as electron donors and interacts with free radicals. On the other hand, the CD-added films showed higher antioxidant activity leading to antioxidant activity within minutes of incubation in both methods. The addition of GCD and NGCD increased the free radical scavenging ability of the CNF film to 98.6 % and 98.7 %, respectively, in the ABTS method and 79.4 % and 84.7 % in the DPPH method. The excellent antioxidant activity of the GCD can be attributed to their surface functional groups that participate in free radical scavenging. The antioxidant activity of the GCD-added film was higher in the ABTS method than in the DPPH method because the swelling rate of the CNF film in the ABTS aqueous solution was higher than in the methanolic DPPH solution. Both CNF/GCD and CNF/NGCD films showed similar antioxidant activity when determined by the ABTS method. However, the CNF/NGCD film showed slightly higher activity when determined by the DPPH method, which could be attributed to the higher releasing rate of NGCD in alcoholic solution as observed in the release test. Previously, Caneschi et al. (2017) has found that the amide/amine group of NGCD reduces the polarity of CD and increases its solubility in alcohol solutions (Caneschi et al., 2017). The antioxidant test results agree well with the GCD release rate depending on the test solution. Due to their high antioxidant activity, these films can be used in active packaging applications to prevent the oxidation of packaged foods.

Antimicrobial packaging plays an essential role in inhibiting pathogenic contamination in foods, thereby extending the shelf-life of foods and securing food safety. The antimicrobial activity of the CNF-based films was evaluated using a total viable colony count method against bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli) and mold (Aspergillus flavus), and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . As expected, the neat CNF film did not show any antimicrobial activity, showing similar microbial growth as the control group. On the other hand, the GCD-incorporated CNF films showed obvious antibacterial activity, which decreased the growth or killed the test microorganisms depending on the types of GCD and test microorganisms. Among the GCD-added CNF films, the NGCD-added CNF film showed stronger antibacterial activity than the GCD-added film. The GCD-added film showed an antibacterial effect to reduce the growth of the test microorganisms, whereas the NGCD-added film showed a clear microbial killing effect. In addition, the CNF/NGCD film inhibited the growth of A. flavus entirely after two d of incubation, indicating that NGCD had a high antifungal function. Therefore, the prepared film can be used for the practical use of antibacterial packaging materials (Al-Tayyar et al., 2020).

The antimicrobial action of the CNF-based film was confirmed through the SEM image of the CNF-based film-treated microorganisms. As shown in the SEM images (Fig. 4 ), GCDs interactions altered the morphology of microorganisms and induced severe cell membrane disruption. The control and CNF film-treated groups showed normal growth with many cells, confirming CNF film has no antibacterial function. On the other hand, there was a large-scale perturbation in the periphery after CNF/NGCD film treatment and severe damage to the bacterial cells due to cytoplasmic flux. In contrast, the CNF/GCD film-treated group showed some damage to the cell surface, and some showed partial damage to the cell wall. In conclusion, the integration of NGCD disrupted the cell membrane structure and lost the integrity of bacteria.

The strong antimicrobial activity of the CNF/NGCD film is probably due to specific functional groups such as amides and amines on the surface of NGCD (Travlou et al., 2018). It has been reported that N-functionalized GCDs have a chemical reaction function related to electron-donating capacity (Stöhr et al., 1991; Strelko et al., 2004). The additional free electrons in NGCD further enhance the electron-donating function, induce oxygen radicals, and enhance electron transport. The protonation of amines/amides groups facilitates the penetration of NGCD into the negatively charged microbial cell walls. It was previously shown that ions in the nitrogen complex act as free radicals, contributing to the inactivation of enzymes that require free hydroxyl groups to perform their functions (El-Ayaan and Abdel-Aziz, 2005).

Depending on the specific functional group, an aqueous environment promotes the reaction to form O2, followed by hydroxide (OH−) and hydroperoxide (HO2-) anions. The ROS, such as O2 −, OH−, and HO2-, attach to the microbial surface by electrostatic forces, interacting with proteins, lipids, and nucleotides to denature membrane proteins that cause serious cellular physiological damage and ultimately increase permeability and DNA proliferation associated with cell lysis (Dwyer et al., 2014; Nigam, 2015). The ROS generating ability of the CNF-based films was measured by ORAC (oxygen radical absorbance capability) method to confirm the efficacy of antibacterial activity of the film. ROS production in the CNF-based films was measured using alkalinized fluorescein to determine the amount of green fluorescence produced after the oxidation of fluorescein (Fig. 4). Test films were soaked in fluorescein solution, and the relative fluorescence amounts were evaluated as a function of time. The ROS production results were calculated using a calibration curve (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 4, the CNF, CNF/GCD, and CNF/NGCD films generated ROS of 0.5 ± 0.1, 3.0 ± 0.3, and 6.1 ± 0.5 mM, respectively. The control fluorescein solution did not yield any ROS within 1 h. However, the fluorescence peak intensity of the solution containing the CNF/GCD film gradually decreased with time, confirming the enhanced ROS generation of GCD. In addition, the low fluorescence signal of CNF/NGCD film compared to CNF/GCD film indicated higher ROS generation over time and reached saturation levels after 60 min. Consequently, both CNF/GCD and CNF/NGCD films showed high antimicrobial efficiencies due to the presence of GCD and NGCD generating ROS in a time-dependent manner.

The use of the films for food packaging or biomedical applications requires investigation of the cytotoxicity of the prepared films. A cell viability test was performed using the MTT test to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of the CNF-based films, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . Cell density slightly increased in neat CNF film at 24 and 48 h, whereas a slight decrease was observed at 72 h (93.2 %). The cell viability of CNF/GCD and CNF/NGCD films decreased down to ∼ 87.2 and 84.2 %. Compared to the CNF/GCD film, nitrogen-functionalized GCD showed slightly lower cell viability (%). The order of cell viability or cytotoxicity of the CNF-based films is consistent with the results of the antimicrobial activity of the films. It is worth noting that more than 84 % of the cells survive for 72 h. The digital optical microscopy images of mouse fibroblast L929 cells after 72 h treatment with CNF-based films are shown in Fig. 5. Overall, the tested films have low toxicity against the mouse fibroblast L929 cell line, indicating very low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Various cytotoxicity studies have confirmed that CDs (with or without surface modification) exhibit very low toxicity (Havrdova et al., 2016). Moreover, cytotoxicity studies of CDs performed to date have shown that in most cases, concentrations of 10−100 μg/mL cause only a slight loss in cell viability (∼10−20 %), which is suitable for cell labeling/staining purposes (Ray et al., 2009) and is also expected to be useful for food packaging applications.

Tangerine and strawberry fruit were coated with CNF film solutions to test the effectiveness of the coating material on extending the shelf life of fruit. The change in the appearance of the fruit during storage is shown in Fig. 6 . All fruit initially showed a fresh appearance and maintained quality until 5 and 2 d of storage for tangerines and strawberries. However, after 10 and 3 d of storage, the uncoated and coated fruit with the neat CNF and CNF/GCD films had mold growth on the surface, which was more severe after 15 and 4 d of storage. On the other hand, tangerines and strawberries coated with the CNF/NGCD film maintained apparent quality without showing any sign of mold growth, mainly due to the antifungal activity of the CNF/NGCD (Figs. 4 and S5). In general, contamination and growth of fungi and pathogenic microorganisms are major causes of spoilage and quality degradation of fruit during storage (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021). The CNF film with added GCD, especially the CNF/NGCD film, has a high potential for active edible coating of fruit to improve shelf life and maintain the quality during storage and distribution after harvest.

Two types of carbon quantum dots (CD), glucose-based CD (GCD) and N-functionalized GCD (NGCD), were prepared using a hydrothermal method. The GCD showed some antimicrobial activity but not a antifungal activity, whereas the NGCD showed effective antibacterial and antifungal activity. Both CDs were used to prepare CNF-based functional films. The addition of CD did not affect the mechanical properties but increased the water vapor permeability, and water contact angle of the CNF film and increased the UV blocking properties without sacrificing the transparency of the film. The CD-added CNF films exhibited high antioxidant activity, showing more than 80 % free radical scavenging against DPPH and 100 % against ABTS. The antimicrobial test revealed an efficient inhibitory effect of CNF/NGCD film against the growth of L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and A. flavus. When the CNF/NGCD film solution was used for tangerine and strawberries coating, it inhibited mold growth on the fruit surface and extended the fruit shelf life by more than 10 and 2 d, respectively. Therefore, the CNF/NGCD coating formulation can be developed as an antifungal coating for fruit susceptible to fungal infections to prolong the shelf life of the fruit after harvest.

Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data Analysis, Writing - draft. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding Acquisition. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data Analysis, Writing - draft. Ruchir Priyadarshi: Writing - draft. Sanghee Han: Investigation.
